text: ADMISSION HISTORY AND PHYSICAL: Patient is a 44-year-old male with a history of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, poorly controlled, presenting with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, polyuria, and progressive lethargy. Vitals: Temp 98.6 F, HR 122, BP 98/60, RR 28, O2 sat 94% on RA. Physical Exam: Patient is cachectic, dry mucous membranes, Kussmaul respirations noted. Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. Labs: Glucose 685 mg/dL, pH 7.02, HCO3 6 mEq/L, Anion Gap 28, Beta-hydroxybutyrate 7.2 mmol/L. Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). Allergy: Patient has a documented, severe anaphylactic reaction to Penicillin (hives, angioedema). Plan: Admit to ICU. Start DKA protocol insulin drip, aggressive fluid resuscitation with 0.9% NS, monitor electrolytes. NEPHROLOGY CONSULT: Consulted for acute kidney injury in the setting of DKA. AKI is likely multifactorial, secondary to severe dehydration and potential osmotic diuresis, but we must rule out acute tubular necrosis. Current urine output is 15 mL/hour. Fractional excretion of sodium is 0.8%. We recommend maintaining fluid resuscitation with careful titration to avoid pulmonary edema. Avoid nephrotoxic agents. Follow creatinine q6 hours. Will reassess tomorrow. PROGRESS NOTE - DAY 2: Patient remains in the ICU. DKA protocol continued; glucose levels are trending downward, currently 240 mg/dL. Anion gap closing to 14. Potassium requires aggressive replacement, as patient is hypokalemic at 3.2 mEq/L. Urine output has improved to 40 mL/hour. Creatinine remains elevated at 2.1 mg/dL. Patient is oriented x3 but complains of generalized weakness. Insulin drip weaning down to subcutaneous transition. Start basal-bolus regimen. Progress note indicates the patient is tolerating clear liquids. Will transfer to step-down unit if stable. PROGRESS NOTE - DAY 4: Patient transferred to medical ward. Insulin requirements have stabilized. Patient reports improved appetite. Creatinine has plateaued at 1.8 mg/dL. Patient remains stable for discharge consideration. Nursing notes indicate patient is ambulating in the halls. Vital signs stable. Blood glucose monitoring protocol continued. DISCHARGE SUMMARY DRAFT: Patient is a 44-year-old male admitted with DKA and AKI. Hospital course was complicated by severe electrolyte imbalances, which were corrected. Renal function has stabilized to a creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL. Patient is now transition to outpatient care. Education regarding insulin self-administration and sick-day management was provided to the patient and family. Discharge medications include: Long-acting insulin glargine 20 units nightly, rapid-acting insulin aspart sliding scale, Lisinopril 5 mg daily for renal protection, and Amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 7 days to address a recent minor skin abrasion. Follow-up appointments scheduled with Primary Care Physician in 1 week and Endocrinology in 2 weeks. Discharge status: Stable. The patient will follow up at the clinic as scheduled for further evaluation of ongoing health maintenance needs. Please ensure the patient maintains adequate hydration at home and monitors blood glucose four times daily.
